content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 31, 1994

Ms. Lois M. Shade
811 E. Juanita Ave.
Glendora, CA 91740

Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-75

Dear Ms. Shade:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTION

If you receive donations to off-set your newsletter
publication costs, are the donations reportable?

CONCLUSIONS

As an elected officeholder, donations received to offset your
newsletter expenses are considered "contributions" and reportable
on a campaign disclosure statement. Payments made to produce the
newsletter are also reportable "expenditures."

FACTS

You are currently serving on the Glendora City Council and
have two years left of your term of office. In response to
comments regarding the lack of information on council business in
your local newspaper, you initiated a newsletter that summarizes
issues and matters before the council. You provided examples of
several newsletters all of which contained articles relating to
matters before the Glendale City Council. Recently, several
people have offered you money to help off-set your publication
costs. Currently, you are paying all the costs. In the near
future, you expect over 125 newsletter subscribers.

1/
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-94-75
Page 2




Payments received for political purposes are defined under
the Act as "contributions." (Section 82015.) Commission
Regulation 18215 provides that unless it is clear that a payment
is made for personal purposes unrelated to your status as an
officeholder, a payment received by you or made at your behest is
considered a "contribution."2/ (Regulation 18215.) According to
the facts, you started the newsletter after you were elected to
the Glenda City Council and the newsletter's focus is on matters
that come before you as an officeholder. Therefore, payments
received by you to help off-set your newsletter costs would be
considered "contributions."

In addition, when an officeholder makes a payment for
political purposes, the payment is defined as an "expenditure."
(Section 82025 and Regulation 18225.) Since it has been
determined that your newsletter is related to your status as a
councilmember and serves a political purpose, payments to publish
the newsletter are considered "expenditures." All contributions
and expenditures must be disclosed on your campaign statements.
(Section 84211.)

If you have any questions regarding this letter, please
contact me at 916/322-5662.

Sincerely,

Steve Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Lynda Cassady
Assistant Division Chief




2/ "Made at the behest" means a payment made under the control
or at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with, or at the request or suggestion of
a candidate, controlled committee... (Regulation 18215(b).)
